perturbation.original_text: ISO shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that each Application completed by an Applicant is current and contains accurate and complete information. ISO's Application Materials shall comply with all applicable Rules, laws and regulations. All Application Materials and any changes to the form and content thereof must be approved in writing by SERVICERS prior to use by ISO, which approval will not be unreasonably withheld or delayed beyond [***] [***] days unless such changes
perturbation.changed_text: ISO shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that each Application completed by an Applicant is current and contains accurate and complete information. ISO's Application Materials shall comply with all applicable Rules. All Application Materials and any changes to the form and content thereof must be approved in writing by SERVICERS prior to use by ISO, which approval will not be unreasonably withheld or delayed beyond [***] [***] days unless such changes
perturbation.explanation: Removing the line requiring compliance with 'laws and regulations' from the Application Materials introduces a legal risk. This allows the ISO to potentially use application materials that violate consumer protection laws, such as those requiring clear disclosure of fees or interest rates.
perturbation.law_explanation: TILA mandates clear and conspicuous disclosure of credit terms, including APR, finance charges, and other relevant information, in consumer credit applications. By removing the requirement for ISO's application materials to comply with all applicable laws and regulations, this modification allows ISO to use application materials that do not meet the TILA's disclosure requirements.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for ISO's application materials to comply with 'laws and regulations.' This creates a potential for non-compliance with consumer credit protection laws. The Law Explanation specifically mentions TILA (Truth in Lending Act) and its disclosure requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes Title 15, Chapter 41 of the US Code, which covers consumer credit protection and encompasses TILA. By removing the compliance requirement, the modified contract allows for application materials that could violate these legal mandates, directly contradicting the purpose of consumer credit protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: ISO shall be responsible for ensuring that the Application Materials, the marketing plans, the Promotional Materials and all services performed by ISO hereunder comply, and remain in compliance with, all applicable Rules, laws and regulations; provided, however, that SERVICERS will in good faith inform ISO of issues or concerns with ISO's materials or practices observed by SERVICERS that SERVICERS reasonably believe may be in violation of applicable Rules. All documents and any changes to the form and content thereof must be approved in writing by SERVICERS prior to use by ISO, which approval will not be unreasonably withheld or delayed beyond fifteen (15) days.
perturbation.changed_text: ISO shall be responsible for ensuring that the Application Materials, the marketing plans, the Promotional Materials and all services performed by ISO hereunder comply, and remain in compliance with, all applicable Rules; provided, however, that SERVICERS will in good faith inform ISO of issues or concerns with ISO's materials or practices observed by SERVICERS that SERVICERS reasonably believe may be in violation of applicable Rules. All documents and any changes to the form and content thereof must be approved in writing by SERVICERS prior to use by ISO, which approval will not be unreasonably withheld or delayed beyond fifteen (15) days.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'laws and regulations' requirement introduces potential non-compliance with consumer protection laws regarding advertising and service provision. This could lead to legal challenges related to deceptive marketing or unfair business practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FTC Act prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By removing the requirement for ISO's materials and services to comply with all applicable laws and regulations, this modification permits ISO to potentially engage in deceptive marketing practices or provide services in a manner that violates the FTC Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Trade Commission Act | Federal Trade Commission Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for ISO's materials and services to comply with 'laws and regulations,' which directly contradicts the FTC Act's prohibition of unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By omitting this compliance requirement, the changed contract allows ISO to potentially violate consumer protection laws, as the FTC Act (Snippet 1) empowers the Commission to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.'

perturbation.original_text: ISO will, at any and all reasonable times, permit SERVICERS' employees, agents, attorneys, auditors, or bank regulators to inspect ISO's place of business to audit its operations for compliance with all Rules, laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency or Bank Card association, all at SERVICERS' expense.
perturbation.changed_text: ISO will, at any and all reasonable times, permit SERVICERS' employees, agents, attorneys, auditors, or bank regulators to inspect ISO's place of business to audit its operations for compliance with all Rules and directives of any Bank Card association, all at SERVICERS' expense.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause related to compliance with 'laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency' restricts the scope of the audit, creating a risk of overlooking violations of consumer financial protection laws. This limits the ability to detect and rectify non-compliant practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Dodd-Frank Act prohibits unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices in connection with any transaction with a consumer for financial product or service. By limiting the audit to only compliance with Rules and Bank Card association directives and by excluding the government laws, rules and regulations, this modification prevents SERVICERS's auditors from being able to detect practices which, although not prohibited by the Rules, could be regarded as violations of federal law under 12 U.S.C. § 5536.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for ISO to permit SERVICERS to audit for compliance with 'laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency.' The Law Explanation states that this omission prevents SERVICERS's auditors from detecting practices that could violate federal law under 12 U.S.C. § 5536. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes "5536. Prohibited acts." as part of the Dodd-Frank Act, which prohibits unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices. By removing the explicit mention of 'laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency,' the modified text weakens the ability to ensure compliance with consumer financial protection laws, directly contradicting the purpose of the Dodd-Frank Act as described in both the Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 2, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
